integrative.title: Integrative Close: System Coordination
integrative.objective: Explain the relationship between a TN-C-S supply arrangement and the distinct roles of bonding and CPCs in achieving ADS.
retrievalTextLines: In a TN-C-S (PME) installation, the electricity supplier provides a combined Neutral and Earth (PEN) conductor to the property, which is then split at the intake.

To ensure Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) works correctly, the electrician must install both Main Protective Bonding to metallic services (like water pipes) and Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs) to every individual circuit.
integrative.questionText: Explain how the Main Protective Bonding and the CPC perform different roles to achieve safety in this TN-C-S system during a fault.
answerGuidance: The TN-C-S system relies on the split PEN conductor at the intake. The Main Protective Bonding connects the metallic water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to maintain an equipotential zone (keeping all metal at the same voltage). In contrast, the CPC provides a dedicated path for fault current from a specific circuit back to the MET and the supplier's return path, which allows the protective device (fuse/MCB) to trip and disconnect the supply.
integrative.hint: Think about which conductor is there to keep pipes at the same voltage and which one is there to carry fault current from an appliance to trip the breaker.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
anchorFacts: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
Why this matters: ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to ensuring safety.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps: Core ADS Components
Protective Devices and Bonding
TT Earthing Systems
TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
CPC and Earthing Conductor

steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the primary components that make up an ADS system.
anchorFacts: Automatic Disconnection of Supply, or ADS, is not a single device but a coordinated safety system designed to cut power instantly during a fault. For ADS to function, several components must work together to create a safe path for electricity and then break the circuit.

The system includes the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which runs to every outlet, and the Earthing Conductor, which connects the whole building to the ground. It also uses Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding to link metal pipes to the earth. Finally, Protective Devices like fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs) act as the 'off switch' that triggers when a fault is detected. In some setups, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the ground) is also required to complete the path. If any of these parts are missing or broken, the safety 'handshake' fails, and a fault could leave metal surfaces live and dangerous.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking ADS is a single device rather than a system of coordinated components.
Think of ADS as a team of parts including conductors, bonding, and breakers working together.
taskConstraints: The list of components including CPC, bonding, earthing conductor, and protective devices.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to ensuring safety.
steps.questionIntent: The list of components including CPC, bonding, earthing conductor, and protective devices.

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is ADS described as a coordinated system rather than just a single circuit breaker?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to ensuring safety.
deeperAnswerGuidance: It requires a safe path and a device to work together; If the CPC or Earthing Conductor is missing the breaker cannot trip; Safety depends on the handshake between conductors and protective devices; A fault remains dangerous if any part of the loop is broken
steps.hint: Think of ADS as a team of parts including conductors, bonding, and breakers working together.

steps.title: Protective Devices and Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors.
anchorFacts: In an ADS system, different components have very specific jobs. Protective devices, such as fuses and MCBs, are designed to carry the normal load current but 'trip' or blow when they sense a massive surge caused by a fault. Their job is to physically disconnect the power.

Bonding conductors have a different purpose. Main Protective Bonding connects metallic services like gas and water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that all touchable metalwork stays at the same electrical potential (voltage). By keeping everything at the same voltage, you prevent a person from receiving a shock if they touch a metal pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time. Unlike a CPC, bonding conductors are not meant to carry fault current from a broken appliance back to the board; they are there to create an 'equipotential' zone where no dangerous voltage difference can exist between metal parts.
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing that bonding conductors carry fault current back to the source like a CPC does.
Protective devices cut the power, while bonding keeps all metal pipes at the same voltage.
taskConstraints: The roles of main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices (fuses/MCBs).
Confusing bonding with circuit protection can lead to incorrect installation and failure of safety systems.
steps.questionIntent: The roles of main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices (fuses/MCBs).

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary job of protective devices like fuses and MCBs?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way bonding prevent an electric shock even if it does not carry fault current from a broken appliance?
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Confusing bonding with circuit protection can lead to incorrect installation and failure of safety systems.
deeperAnswerGuidance: It keeps all touchable metalwork at the same electrical potential; It prevents a voltage difference between two metal surfaces; It ensures a person cannot become a path for current between pipes and appliances; It maintains the same voltage across all metallic services
steps.hint: Protective devices cut the power, while bonding keeps all metal pipes at the same voltage.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
anchorFacts: The letters used to describe earthing systems tell us exactly how the installation is connected to the ground. In a TT system, the first T stands for 'Terre' (the French word for Earth), meaning the source of energy is connected to Earth. The second T means the consumer's metalwork is also connected to Earth via a local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. Instead, the consumer must provide their own means of earthing by driving an Earth Electrode, usually a copper-plated steel rod, directly into the soil. Because soil has a much higher resistance than a copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often too low to trip a standard fuse or MCB quickly. To solve this, TT systems almost always require a Residual Current Device (RCD) to ensure the supply is disconnected fast enough to prevent a fatal shock.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming all earthing systems use the supplier's cable sheath for the earth return path.
In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the user must provide their own earth rod.
taskConstraints: The use of a local earth electrode (earth rod) instead of a supplier-provided earth terminal.
TT systems have higher resistance paths and require different protection strategies (like RCDs) compared to TN systems.
steps.questionIntent: The use of a local earth electrode (earth rod) instead of a supplier-provided earth terminal.

steps.deeperQuestionIntent: TT systems have higher resistance paths and require different protection strategies (like RCDs) compared to TN systems.
deeperAnswerGuidance: Soil has a much higher resistance than copper wire; The fault current is often too low to trip a standard breaker; The resistance of the earth electrode path limits the current flow; An RCD is needed to detect the small imbalance that a fuse would ignore
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the user must provide their own earth rod.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.
anchorFacts: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth return path, but they do it in two different ways. In a TN-S system, the S stands for 'Separate.' This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are kept as two distinct, separate wires all the way from the substation transformer to the consumer's property. The earth path is often provided by the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the C stands for 'Combined' and the S stands for 'Separate.' In this arrangement, the supplier saves money by combining the Neutral and Earth into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) for the journey through the street. However, when the cable reaches the consumer's house, these are split back into a separate Neutral terminal and an Earth terminal at the cutout. This system is also known as Protective Multiple Earthing (PME) because the combined conductor is connected to earth at multiple points along the network.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking TN-C-S (PME) has a separate earth wire all the way back to the transformer.
S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Neutral and Earth share a wire in the street.
taskConstraints: TN-S (Separate Neutral and Earth) vs TN-C-S (Combined Neutral and Earth in the supply).
The supply type dictates how the consumer's earthing terminal is connected and affects the safety of the installation.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
anchorFacts: It is vital to use the correct names for conductors because they perform different roles in the ADS system. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the green-and-yellow wire that belongs to an individual circuit. For example, the CPC in a kitchen socket circuit only protects that specific circuit by connecting the metal casing of the toaster or kettle to the consumer unit.

The Earthing Conductor is the single, main wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (such as the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod). If the CPC is like a 'bodyguard' for one specific circuit, the Earthing Conductor is the 'main bridge' that carries all potential fault currents from the whole building into the ground. You will only have one main Earthing Conductor per installation, but you will have many CPCs—one for every circuit you install.
misconceptionsToWatch: The CPC is for a single circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house.
steps.questionIntent: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.

deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC is the dedicated bodyguard for that specific circuit
Main Protective Bonding is only for metallic services like gas and water

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying a TN-C-S System and its ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify a specific earthing system from visual evidence and list the components required to ensure Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property built in the 1990s. At the service head (the cutout), you see a single incoming cable. Inside the cutout, the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a separate neutral and a separate earth wire run to the consumer unit.

Step 1: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the Neutral and Earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the consumer's intake, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' system. Using our codes, this is a TN-C-S system (also known as PME).
Step 2: Identify the main link to the earth source. The 16mm² green-and-yellow cable running from the cutout's earth terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.

Step 3: Identify the circuit-level protection. Every individual circuit (like the upstairs lights or kitchen sockets) has its own green-and-yellow wire. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).

Step 4: Identify the bonding requirements. You locate the copper gas and water pipes entering the building. To maintain an equipotential zone, you must connect these to the MET using Main Protective Bonding conductors.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property built in the 1990s. At the service head (the cutout), you see a single incoming cable. Inside the cutout, the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a separate neutral and a separate earth wire run to the consumer unit.
steps: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the Neutral and Earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the consumer's intake, this is a 'Combined' then 'Separate' system. Using our codes, this is a TN-C-S system (also known as PME).
Identify the main link to the earth source. The 16mm² green-and-yellow cable running from the cutout's earth terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Identify the circuit-level protection. Every individual circuit (like the upstairs lights or kitchen sockets) has its own green-and-yellow wire. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).

Identify the bonding requirements. You locate the copper gas and water pipes entering the building. To maintain an equipotential zone, you must connect these to the MET using Main Protective Bonding conductors.

Identify the disconnection component. In the consumer unit, you find Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs). These are the Protective Devices that will actually 'trip' to provide the 'Automatic Disconnection' part of ADS.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: ADS is a team effort. If the CPC is missing, the fault current can't reach the board; if the Earthing Conductor is missing, the current can't reach the source. Both must be present for the protective device to do its job.

steps.hint: Remember: 'C' is for Combined (in the street) and 'S' is for Separate (in the house). Always trace the path from the appliance back to the supplier's terminal.

steps.title: Guided Practice: Identifying a TN-S System
steps.objective: Identify a TN-S earthing system and its primary link to the earth source based on a technical scenario.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are working in an older industrial unit. The incoming supply cable has a lead sheath.
The earth connection is made via a metal clamp on this lead sheath.
The Neutral and Earth conductors are kept entirely separate from the transformer all the way to the consumer's installation.
A green-and-yellow conductor connects the lead sheath clamp to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

steps.title: Independent Practice: TT Systems
steps.objective: Identify a TT earthing system and the component used to establish a local earth connection.
anchorFacts: Scenario: A remote agricultural building is being wired.
The electricity supplier provides a Phase and Neutral but does not provide an earth terminal.
To protect the installation, the electrician drives a copper-clad steel rod into the ground outside the building.
This rod provides the connection to the mass of Earth for the entire installation.

steps.title: Integrative Close: System Coordination
steps.objective: Explain the relationship between a TN-C-S supply arrangement and the distinct roles of bonding and CPCs in achieving ADS.
anchorFacts: In a TN-C-S (PME) installation, the electricity supplier provides a combined Neutral and Earth (PEN) conductor to the property, which is then split at the intake.

To ensure Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) works correctly, the electrician must install both Main Protective Bonding to metallic services (like water pipes) and Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs) to every individual circuit.

        "In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal. parsed.summary: The repair plan focuses on correcting contaminated answer guidance in Step 2 and significantly expanding the technical depth of the 'anchorFacts' across all teaching steps to satisfy 'Teaching Before Testing' and 'Alignment to LO' requirements. This ensures the AI tutor has enough grounded material to explain concepts before questioning the learner.

fixes.problem: The question asks for the 'technical term for a zone', but the answer guidance provides the name of the conductor (Main Protective Bonding) instead of the zone itself.
fixes.repairInstruction: Change the answerGuidance to ["Equipotential zone", "Equipotential"] to match the specific term requested in the question text.

fixes.problem: Teaching anchors are too thin, providing only a high-level overview without defining the specific components the learner is immediately tested on.
fixes.repairInstruction: Expand anchorFacts to include technical definitions for: CPC (circuit-specific protection), Earthing Conductor (MET to earth source), Main Protective Bonding (services to MET), and Protective Devices (MCBs/Fuses for disconnection).

fixes.problem: The distinction between bonding and protection is described conceptually but lacks the technical 'why' needed for a strong electrical tutor tone.
fixes.repairInstruction: Add to anchorFacts: 'Bonding does not provide a path for fault current to trip a breaker; its sole purpose is to eliminate potential difference between touchable parts. Protective devices (MCBs) rely on the low-resistance path provided by the CPC to detect faults.'

fixes.problem: TT system teaching is missing the critical technical reason why RCDs are used (Earth Loop Impedance/Ra values).
fixes.repairInstruction: Update anchorFacts to explain that soil resistance is significantly higher than copper, meaning fault current is often too low to operate an MCB, necessitating an RCD to detect small leakage currents for ADS.

fixes.problem: TN-C-S explanation is missing the technical definition of the PEN conductor.
fixes.repairInstruction: Add to anchorFacts: 'In TN-C-S, the supplier uses a PEN (Protective Earth Neutral) conductor. The "Multiple" in PME refers to the supplier earthing this conductor at several points along the distribution network to ensure safety if a break occurs.'

fixes.problem: The comparison between CPC and Earthing Conductor is purely metaphorical ('bodyguard' vs 'bridge') and needs physical context.
fixes.repairInstruction: Add physical identifiers to anchorFacts: 'The Earthing Conductor is typically a single 16mm² cable in domestic settings, while CPCs are sized according to the specific circuit they protect (e.g., 1.5mm² or 2.5mm²).'

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

parsed.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
parsed.subject: C&G 2365
parsed.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
anchorFacts: Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is the primary method of protection against electric shock in most installations.
To achieve ADS, we use specific earthing arrangements: TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S.
The system relies on a coordinated team of components: the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), Earthing Conductor, Main Protective Bonding, and Protective Devices (like MCBs).
Understanding these parts is the first step to ensuring an installation is safe and compliant with BS 7671.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps: Core ADS Components
Protective Devices and Bonding
TT Earthing Systems
TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
CPC and Earthing Conductor

steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the primary components that make up an ADS system.
anchorFacts: Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is a safety system designed to cut power instantly during a fault. It is not a single device, but a coordinated loop.
The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) runs to every outlet and appliance, providing a dedicated path for fault current.
The Earthing Conductor is the main link that connects the building's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the ground or supplier terminal).
Main Protective Bonding and Supplementary Bonding link metallic services (like gas and water pipes) to the earth to prevent dangerous voltage differences.
Protective Devices, such as fuses or Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), act as the 'off switch' that triggers when the fault current flows through the loop.
In some setups, an Earth Electrode (a metal rod in the ground) is required to complete the path to the mass of Earth.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking ADS is a single device rather than a system of coordinated components.
Think of ADS as a team of parts including conductors, bonding, and breakers working together.
taskConstraints: The list of components including CPC, bonding, earthing conductor, and protective devices.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to ensuring safety.
steps.questionIntent: Identify the primary components of ADS and their basic roles.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain that ADS requires both a path (conductors) and a device (breaker) to function.

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is ADS described as a coordinated system rather than just a single circuit breaker?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It requires a safe path and a device to work together; If the CPC or Earthing Conductor is missing the breaker cannot trip; Safety depends on the handshake between conductors and protective devices; A fault remains dangerous if any part of the loop is broken
steps.hint: Think of ADS as a team of parts including conductors, bonding, and breakers working together.

steps.title: Protective Devices and Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors.
anchorFacts: Protective devices (MCBs and fuses) are designed to carry normal current but disconnect the supply rapidly during an overcurrent or fault. They rely on a low-resistance path (the CPC) to allow enough current to flow to trigger them.

Bonding conductors have a different purpose: they do not provide a path for fault current to trip a breaker. Instead, they ensure all touchable metalwork stays at the same electrical potential (voltage).
Main Protective Bonding connects metallic services like gas and water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

By keeping everything at the same voltage, you create an 'equipotential zone'. This prevents a person from receiving a shock if they touch a metal pipe and a faulty appliance at the same time, as there is no voltage difference between them.
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing that bonding conductors carry fault current back to the source like a CPC does.
Protective devices cut the power, while bonding keeps all metal pipes at the same voltage.
taskConstraints: The roles of main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, and protective devices (fuses/MCBs).
Confusing bonding with circuit protection can lead to incorrect installation and failure of safety systems.
steps.questionIntent: Distinguish between the roles of bonding (voltage equalization) and protective devices (disconnection).
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the concept of eliminating potential difference.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary job of protective devices like fuses and MCBs?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way bonding prevents an electric shock even if it does not carry fault current from a broken appliance?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It keeps all touchable metalwork at the same electrical potential; It prevents a voltage difference between two metal surfaces; It ensures a person cannot become a path for current between pipes and appliances; It maintains the same voltage across all metallic services
steps.hint: Protective devices cut the power, while bonding keeps all metal pipes at the same voltage.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
anchorFacts: The letters in earthing codes describe the connection. The first letter 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), meaning the source (transformer) is connected to Earth.
The second letter 'T' means the consumer's metalwork is connected to Earth via a local connection, not through the supplier's cable.
In a TT system, the consumer must provide their own means of earthing by driving an Earth Electrode (usually a copper-plated steel rod) into the soil.
Because soil has a much higher resistance than copper wire, the fault current in a TT system is often too low to trip a standard MCB or fuse quickly.
To achieve ADS in a TT system, a Residual Current Device (RCD) is almost always required to detect small leakage currents and disconnect the supply fast enough.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming all earthing systems use the supplier's cable sheath for the earth return path.
In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the user must provide their own earth rod.
taskConstraints: The use of a local earth electrode (earth rod) instead of a supplier-provided earth terminal.
TT systems have higher resistance paths and require different protection strategies (like RCDs) compared to TN systems.

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is a standard fuse or MCB often unable to provide ADS on its own in a TT system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Soil has a much higher resistance than copper wire; The fault current is often too low to trip a standard breaker; The resistance of the earth electrode path limits the current flow; An RCD is needed to detect the small imbalance that a fuse would ignore
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the user must provide their own earth rod.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.
anchorFacts: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide the earth return path. The 'N' means the consumer's earth is connected to the supplier's Neutral.

In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate'. The Neutral and Earth conductors are kept as two distinct wires all the way from the substation to the consumer. The earth path is often the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined'. The supplier combines the Neutral and Earth into a single conductor, called a PEN (Protective Earth Neutral) conductor, for the journey through the street.

At the consumer's property, the PEN conductor is split into separate Neutral and Earth terminals. This system is also known as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing) because the supplier earths the PEN conductor at multiple points along the network.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking TN-C-S (PME) has a separate earth wire all the way back to the transformer.
S is for Separate; C is for Combined. Look at whether the Neutral and Earth share a wire in the street.
taskConstraints: TN-S (Separate Neutral and Earth) vs TN-C-S (Combined Neutral and Earth in the supply).
The supply type dictates how the consumer's earthing terminal is connected and affects the safety of the installation.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
anchorFacts: The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the green-and-yellow wire belonging to an individual circuit. It connects the metal casing of appliances on that specific circuit back to the consumer unit.
The Earthing Conductor is the single, main wire that connects the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (the supplier's terminal or earth rod).
In a typical domestic setting, the Earthing Conductor is usually a 16mm² cable, whereas CPCs are smaller, sized to match the circuit they protect (e.g., 1.5mm² or 2.5mm²).
You will have many CPCs in a building—one for every circuit—but only one main Earthing Conductor per installation.
misconceptionsToWatch: The CPC is for a single circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house.

steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Clarify that bonding is for services, while CPC is for circuit faults.

The CPC connects the appliance casing directly to the earthing terminal
Bonding does not provide the fault return path for individual appliances
steps.hint: The CPC is for a single circuit; the Earthing Conductor is the main link for the whole house.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying a TN-C-S System and its ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify a specific earthing system from visual evidence and list the components required to ensure Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property. At the cutout, you see a single incoming cable. Inside, the supplier has provided an earth terminal linked to the neutral block. From here, separate neutral and earth wires run to the consumer unit.
Step 1: Identify the supply. Because Neutral and Earth are combined in the street but split at the intake, this is a TN-C-S system (PME).
Step 2: Identify the main link. The 16mm² cable from the cutout to the MET is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 3: Identify circuit protection. Each circuit has a green-and-yellow wire; these are the CPCs.
Step 4: Identify bonding. The gas and water pipes are connected to the MET via Main Protective Bonding conductors.
Step 5: Identify disconnection. The MCBs in the consumer unit are the Protective Devices that provide the 'Automatic Disconnection'.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are inspecting a domestic property built in the 1990s. At the service head (the cutout), you see a single incoming cable. Inside the cutout, the supplier has provided an earth terminal that is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a separate neutral and a separate earth wire run to the consumer unit.
steps: Identify the supply arrangement. Because the Neutral and Earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the consumer's intake, this is a TN-C-S system (PME).
Identify the main link to the earth source. The 16mm² green-and-yellow cable running from the cutout's earth terminal to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Identify the circuit-level protection. Every individual circuit has its own green-and-yellow wire. These are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).
Identify the bonding requirements. You connect the copper gas and water pipes to the MET using Main Protective Bonding conductors.
Identify the disconnection component. In the consumer unit, the MCBs act as the Protective Devices to provide Automatic Disconnection.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: ADS is a team effort. If the CPC is missing, the fault current can't reach the board; if the Earthing Conductor is missing, the current can't reach the source. Both must be present for the protective device to do its job.

steps.hint: Remember: 'C' is for Combined (in the street) and 'S' is for Separate (in the house). Always trace the path from the appliance back to the supplier's terminal.

steps.title: Guided Practice: Identifying a TN-S System
steps.objective: Identify a TN-S earthing system and its primary link to the earth source based on a technical scenario.
anchorFacts: In a TN-S system, the Neutral and Earth are separate all the way from the transformer.
The earth connection is often made via a clamp on the lead sheath of the incoming cable.
The conductor connecting this clamp to the MET is the Earthing Conductor.
steps.questionIntent: Identify a TN-S system from a description of separate conductors and a lead sheath connection.

taskSkeleton.scenario: Scenario: You are working in an older industrial unit. The incoming supply cable has a lead sheath. The earth connection is made via a metal clamp on this lead sheath. The Neutral and Earth conductors are kept entirely separate from the transformer all the way to the consumer's installation.
steps: Observe the lead sheath clamp.
Note that Neutral and Earth are never combined.
Identify the system type.

steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Remember: 'S' stands for Separate. If the earth is provided by the cable sheath and never touches the neutral, it is TN-S.
